symptoms.symptom_name: Profound disabling fatigue with leaden/heavy limbs

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hallmark of a severe ME/CFS crash — disabling fatigue with leaden limbs.

symptoms.symptom_name: Orthostatic intolerance — dizziness and pounding heartbeat on sitting up

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Orthostatic intolerance / dysautonomia is common and prominent in ME/CFS crashes.
Positional tachycardia and dizziness are also signs of volume depletion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Measured orthostatic tachycardia (HR 68 supine rising to 105 on sitting; standing deferred for syncope risk)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A ~37 bpm rise supports volume depletion and/or dysautonomia.
Consistent with the dysautonomia of an ME/CFS crash — sealed clinician measurement, not obtainable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-restorative hypersomnia (~16 hours of unrefreshing sleep)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Unrefreshing sleep is a core ME/CFS feature, prominent in crashes.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of fever or infective symptoms

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No fever or urinary symptoms lowers, but does not exclude, occult infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of melena, haematemesis or shock pallor

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No melena, haematemesis or acute shock pallor argues strongly against acute GI bleed / severe anaemia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of bulbar, fatiguable or focal neurological weakness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Generalised constant heaviness without bulbar or fatiguable weakness argues strongly against myasthenic crisis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced oral intake with reduced urine output and dry mouth

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reduced intake plus reduced output and dry mucosa indicates evolving dehydration.
Poor intake and losses raise the chance of an electrolyte disturbance.

symptoms.symptom_name: Lives alone, strictly bedbound, unable to self-hydrate, high fall risk

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Functional/welfare context — does not discriminate the diagnosis but is the key driver of disposition and safety-netting.

symptoms.symptom_name: Vital signs (clinician-measured): BP 100/65 supine, afebrile 36.5°C, RR 14, SpO2 98%

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Haemodynamically stable, afebrile and normally saturated — consistent with a crash rather than acute organ failure.
Afebrile with stable observations makes overt sepsis less likely, though does not exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Mildly dry mucous membranes on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dry mucous membranes support mild-to-moderate dehydration (clinician exam finding; sealed).

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Leaden fatigue, orthostatic intolerance and unrefreshing hypersomnia together form the recognisable severe-crash constellation of the known illness.
Evolving dehydration in a bedbound patient who lives alone and cannot self-hydrate — the combination, not either alone, is what mandates urgent in-person support brought to the home.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Postural (lying/standing) blood pressure and heart rate
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Quantify the orthostatic drop/dysautonomia and volume status; requires in-person measurement (standing deferred remotely for syncope risk).
Screen for occult UTI despite absent urinary symptoms — a common silent driver of a crash in this group.
Exclude anaemia and quantify dehydration/AKI and electrolyte derangement — the mimics that cannot be excluded remotely; drives the in-person disposition.
No focal deficit or bulbar features — routine imaging is over-investigation and not indicated for this crash.
